ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn provides a generic 'chicken soup' class (FOODON_03302050); no specific Indian hot-and-sour chicken soup subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers chicken soup generically. No specific hot-and-sour Indian variant exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy; closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept specifically covers Indian hot and sour chicken soup. SNOMED CT does not provide sufficient granularity for this dish variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of hot and sour chicken soup is approximately 300 g (one bowl), containing bone-in or boneless chicken pieces in a spiced, acidulated broth.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken soup, home-prepared — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), adapted for hot-and-sour spiced variant
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for home-prepared chicken soup (clear broth with chicken). Values adapted for a hot-and-sour variant by accounting for additional spices (chilli, tamarind/vinegar, ginger, garlic) which contribute negligible macronutrient change at typical quantities. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect restaurant-level seasoning. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Soup, chicken broth, home-prepared' (NDB 06172) used as cross-reference for micronutrient estimates.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soups and broth-based dishes — analogous estimate based on clear chicken soup and spiced broth entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian hot and sour chicken soup. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for clear chicken soup and similar low-carbohydrate spiced broths, which consistently fall in the GI 25–35 range. The dish is predominantly protein and water with minimal starch, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 1.35, rounded to 1.4.

hypertension.guidance: At approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (960 mg per 300 g serving), this soup contributes a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 30, this soup produces a negligible glycemic load per serving (approximately 1.4), making it a suitable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when sodium is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The moderate sodium level (~960 mg per serving) and low saturated fat content (0.5 g per 100 g) present a mixed cardiovascular profile; the lean protein from chicken is beneficial, but sodium reduction through home preparation or restaurant modification is recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot and Sour Chicken Soup is a spiced, acidulated Indian chicken broth dish featuring tender chicken pieces simmered in a flavourful stock seasoned with green chillies, black pepper, ginger, garlic, onion, tomato, cumin, coriander, and an acidic agent such as tamarind or vinegar. The soup delivers a characteristic dual flavour profile — sharp sourness balanced against pungent heat — with a thin, clear-to-lightly-cloudy broth and soft chicken texture. It is commonly served as a starter or light meal in Indian restaurants across Singapore, including establishments such as Anjana Kitchen and Indian House/Dumpukht Grills & Curry. Nutritionally, the dish is low in carbohydrates (~1.5 g per 100 g), moderate in protein (~4.2 g per 100 g), and low in fat (~1.8 g per 100 g), yielding approximately 38 kcal per 100 g. Its estimated glycemic index of 30 places it firmly in the low-GI category, making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. The primary clinical concern is sodium, with restaurant preparations typically delivering 900–1000 mg per bowl, which warrants attention for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets when prepared with halal-certified chicken.

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry exists for Indian hot and sour chicken soup as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN home-prepared chicken soup data with cross-reference to USDA SR Legacy chicken broth values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct match for this specific dish.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in home-prepared Indian chicken soup per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy cross-reference. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for clear chicken soup and low-carbohydrate spiced broths. Sodium value of 320 mg per 100 g reflects a restaurant-level estimate; home-prepared versions may be lower. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant bowl. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (30 × 1.5 × 3) / 100 = 1.35, rounded to 1.4. This dish is served at two Singapore field survey restaurants: Anjana Kitchen (Six Battery Road) and Indian House/Dumpukht Grills & Curry (Boat Quay), with prices ranging from SGD 7.43 to SGD 12.75.
